title: Lepick Line — Confirmed Trace: Bohemian Immigrant to Hunter Spence
methodology_note: Sources: WikiTree Lepik-8 (Frank Lepik primary profile, confirmed Bohemian origin, immigration 1881); WikiTree Lepick-1 (Jennive Lepick, confirmed Wilson County TX birth 1923); WikiTree Lepick-2 (Fred Charles Lepick Sr., confirmed Brown County KS origin 1894); WikiTree Mikeska-77 (Josef Mikeska, Moravian origin confirmed); WikiTree Mikeska-78 (Mary Mikeska Lepick, Bohemian origin confirmed); WikiTree Mikeska-20 (Petr Mikeska, specific village: Zádveřice, Holešov, Moravia); Kansas State Census 1895 (FamilySearch ark:/61903/3:1:3Q9M-CS3B-RSJ8-H); US Federal Census 1900 Brown County KS (ark:/61903/3:1:S3HT-6LD4-QVJ, records immigration year 1881); US Federal Census 1910 Arkansas (ark:/61903/3:1:33S7-9RVL-147); US Federal Census 1930 Wilson County TX (ark:/61903/3:1:33SQ-GRCQ-9WW); US Federal Census 1940 Bexar County TX (ark:/61903/3:1:3QS7-89MY-XWJP); Kansas County Marriages Brown County Book D image 59 (ark:/61903/3:1:939J-TCSZ-CM); Fred Charles Lepick Jr. obituary, Legacy.com pid=180605817; Dignity Memorial obituary Fred Charles Lepick Jr.; FindAGrave memorials 140131220 (Frank Lepick), 55217353 (Fred C. Lepick Sr.), 36080759 (Jennive L. Mattingly); Kansas Historical Open Content newspapers (Atchison Daily Patriot 1884-04-29, Everest Reflector 1885-01-15, Horton Headlight 1889-02-08, Horton Commercial 1895-01-17); KdeJsme.cz 2016 Czech surname distribution data (Lepík: 88 total, Frýdek-Místek holds 54.5% of all Czech bearers); forebears.io Lepík: 88 bearers, 100% in Czech Republic; FamilySearch Northern Moravia Opava Archive Church Records collection (1571-1905, 206,549 images); CGSI 'The Hrenchir Family and Other Moravians of Brown County, Kansas' (Joan M. Hrenchir, 1997, confirms Sviadnov, Frýdek-Místek District as Brown County Czech immigrant origin). Prior research files 29-lepick-line.json and 44-lepik-bohemia-deep.json fully integrated.

USA 1881, d. 1939 Brown County KS)

person.parents_path_to_confirm: Priority 1: Frank Lepick 1939 Kansas death certificate (Kansas State Archives, Topeka; fee ~$15; would name parents and exact birth place). Priority 2: Brown County naturalization papers 1881–1905 (FamilySearch microfilm or KSHS). Priority 3: Hamburg/Bremen emigration lists 1880–1882. Priority 4: Czech parish records Frýdek-Místek area (FamilySearch Northern Moravia, Opava Archive collection, 206,549 images, online free).

village_of_origin.working_hypothesis: Frýdek-Místek area, Moravian-Silesian Region, Czech Republic

evidence_summary.detail: Forebears.io confirms Lepík (Czech-accented) is 100% concentrated in Czech Republic with exactly 88 bearers globally. KdeJsme.cz 2016 data shows 54.5% of all Czech Lepík bearers reside in Frýdek-Místek municipality, 12.5% in Frýdlant nad Ostravicí (8km south), 14.8% in Ostrava — totaling ~82% in the Moravian-Silesian corridor. This extreme geographic clustering indicates a single-family origin in the Frýdek-Místek district.

Multiple Czech families documented in Brown County, Kansas originate from the Frýdek-Místek / Moravian-Silesian area. The Hrenchir family (subject of the 1997 book 'The Hrenchir Family and Other Moravians of Brown County, Kansas' by Joan M. Hrenchir) traces to Sviadnov, Moravia — located in present-day Frýdek-Místek District. Frank Lepik settled in the same Brown County community and married into the Mikeska family at the same Catholic parish.

Mary Mikeska's family traces to Moravia. WikiTree Mikeska-77 (Josef Mikeska, 1830–1902) is confirmed Moravian-born. WikiTree Mikeska-20 (Petr Mikeska, 1793–1864) documents Zádveřice, Holešov District, Moravia as the origin village — this is in the Zlín Region / SE Moravia, not Frýdek-Místek. Chain migration pattern: both families arrived in Brown County KS, married at St. Joseph Catholic Church Everest. If Frank also originated from Moravia/Silesia corridor, Frýdek-Místek (Lepík surname hotspot) is the strongest candidate.

Czech lep- root means 'to glue/stick' (lepidlo = glue). Possible occupational origin as maker of sticky birdcatching traps or craftsman with adhesive materials. Frank's lifelong shoemaking trade (cobbling requires adhesive) is consistent. Surname likely originated as a localized family name in Frýdek-Místek area within last 400–500 years rather than a widely distributed occupational name.

US census label 'Bohemia, Austria' is a generic designation applied to all Czech-speaking immigrants — Bohemia, Moravia, and Czech Silesia alike. Per Czech American Wikipedia and standard genealogical practice, Frank's census birthplace does not exclude Moravia or Silesia.

candidate_villages.note: Largest Lepík surname concentration in Czech Republic (54.5% of all bearers). Historically divided between Moravia (Místek) and Czech Silesia (Frýdek) at the Ostravice River; both parts were Austria-Hungary as of 1862.
candidate_villages.confidence: POSSIBLE — best candidate by surname distribution

candidate_villages.note: 8 km south of Frýdek-Místek; 12.5% of Czech Lepík bearers. FamilySearch parish records: 1695–present.

Connected by the Mikeska line — WikiTree Mikeska-62 documents Alžběta Mikeska born 'Jablunce, Moravia' (possibly Jablunkov or Jablůnka). FamilySearch has digitized Jablunkov RC church records 1617–1903.
candidate_villages.confidence: POSSIBLE — tertiary candidate from Mikeska co-migration pattern

wife_line_notable.name: Mary Mikeska (married: Mary Lepick)
wife_line_notable.note: Wife of the immigrant Frank Lepik; her father Josef Mikeska (WikiTree Mikeska-77) is a confirmed Moravian immigrant (1830–1902, arr. USA 1871). The Mikeska family's specific Czech village is Zádveřice, Holešov District, Moravia (documented via WikiTree Mikeska-20, Petr Mikeska 1793–1864, miller of Zádveřice). The Mikeska line in Texas became prominent — Petr Mikeska's descendants established substantial Czech-Texan farming communities.

czech_origin_research_state.surname_distribution_finding: Lepík (Czech-accented) has exactly 88 bearers globally, all in Czech Republic. This is one of the most geographically concentrated rare Czech surnames. KdeJsme.cz 2016 data confirms 54.5% reside in Frýdek-Místek municipality, with an additional ~27% in adjacent Frýdlant nad Ostravicí and Ostrava — total ~82% within a 30km radius. This level of concentration for a surname with only 88 Czech bearers strongly indicates single-family origin in the Frýdek-Místek district at some point in the 16th–18th centuries.

opava_archive_access.collection: Czech Republic, Northern Moravia, Opava Archive Church Books, 1571–1905
opava_archive_access.coverage: 206,549 images; covers Frýdek-Místek District parishes including Místek, Frýdek, Frýdlant nad Ostravicí, Jablunkov
opava_archive_access.access_status: IMAGES AVAILABLE ONLINE (browse only, no surname index)
opava_archive_access.search_strategy: Browse Frýdek-Místek area parishes (Catholic) in FamilySearch Northern Moravia collection for Lepík baptisms 1855–1870 (would find Frank's birth record ~1862) and 1820–1845 (would find Frank's father's birth record)

czech_origin_research_state.naturalization_finding: Frank Lepik does NOT appear in the Topeka Division, District of Kansas naturalization declarations index (National Archives) — suggesting he either naturalized via county court (pre-1906 practice, records at Brown County courthouse or KSHS microfilm) or the federal records are incomplete for this period.
